Once upon a midnight dreary,
while I pondered weak and weary,
Over many a quaint and curious volume of forgotten lore,
While I nodded, nearly napping, suddenly there came a tapping,

Tis some visitor, I muttered, tapping at my chamber door - Only this, and nothing more.

You know, I hate to spoil all the fun you're having. Vance tried to look at Moreau, but he could barely see through the swelling of his puffy eyelids. But I've got some unsettling news. Better enjoy this while you can.

column3:
My grandfather Arthur once took me fishing. I was seven.
I suppose he must have gone fishing frequently, although I never recall him bringing back any fish, nor indeed, any other fishing trips.
He woke me up at five, before the sun was up.

我祖父亞瑟曾帶我去釣魚。那時我七歲。
我想他一定經常去釣魚，雖然我從不記得他帶回任何魚，甚至也不記得其他釣魚行程。
他在五點把我叫醒，那時太陽還沒升起。

column3: Thursday morning. The day of. Billy gets up at five. He eats toast with a glass of water to wash it down. No coffee. No caffeine of any kind until the job is done. When he shoulders the 700 and looks through the Leupold scope, he wants his hands perfectly steady.
column4: 星期四早晨。行動當天。比利五點起床。他吃吐司配一杯水吞下。不喝咖啡。在任務完成前不攝取任何咖啡因。當他扛起700型步槍並透過Leupold瞄準鏡時，他希望雙手完全穩定。

column3:
Not even a yawn. I wasn’t remotely sleepy. I could tell my sense of balance was off—I nearly fell over when I tried to stand up, but I pushed through it and tidied up for a while, sliding the videocassettes into their cases and putting them back on the shelf. I thought some activity might tire me out. I took a Zyprexa and some more Ativan. I ate a handful of melatonin, chewing like a cow on cud. Nothing was working.
So I called Trevor.
“It’s five in the morning,” he said. He sounded irritated and foggy, but he’d answered.
column4:
連打哈欠都沒有。我一點也不困。我能感覺到自己的平衡感失常——當我試圖站起來時差點跌倒，但我還是撐了過去，整理了一會兒，把錄影帶滑入盒中並放回架上。我想做點事或許能讓我疲倦。我吃了一顆Zyprexa和更多Ativan。我嚼了一把褪黑激素，像牛反芻一樣。完全沒有效。
於是我打給特雷弗。
「現在是早上五點，」他說。他的聲音帶著不耐與迷糊，但還是接了電話。

column3:
Sixsmith,
Shot myself through the roof of my mouth at five A.M. this morning with V.A.‘s Luger. But I saw you, my dear, dear fellow! How touched I am that you care so much! On the belfry’s lookout, yesterday, at sunset. Sheerest fluke you didn’t see me first.
column4:
Sixsmith，
今天早上五點，我用V.A.的魯格手槍朝自己口腔上方開了一槍。但我看見你了，我親愛的朋友！你如此關心我，讓我多麼感動！昨天日落時，在鐘樓的瞭望台上。純粹是運氣你沒有先看到我。

column3: I did Danièle’s math in my head. “If we start at ten, walk for four hours, rest for one, walk for another two, that’s seven hours in total. That will take us to five in the morning. Seven hours back, it won’t be noon until we resurface.”

column3: Soon after 5 a.m. he noticed the sky at its edges beginning to turn an oyster grey. Gradually the dark crests of the wooded pine hills emerged, serrated like a saw’s teeth against the spreading light, while in the valleys the white mist seemed as solid as a glacier.

column3: It was in the township of Dunwich, in a large and hardly inhabited farmhouse set against a hillside 4 miles from the village and a mile and a half from any other dwelling, that Wilbur Whately was born at 5 a.m. on Sunday, 2 February, 1913. column3: There are worse things than having behaved foolishly in public. There are worse things than these miniature betrayals, committed or endured or suspected; there are worse things than not being able to sleep for thinking about them. It is 5 a.m. All the worse things come stalking in and stand icily about the bed looking worse and worse and worse.
column4: 在人前做出愚蠢行為並不是最糟的事。比起這些微小的背叛——無論是犯下、承受或懷疑——還有更糟的事；比起因為想著它們而無法入睡，還有更糟的事。現在是早上五點。所有更糟的事都悄然逼近，冷冷地圍在床邊，看起來越來越糟、越來越糟、越來越糟。

column3: What causes young people to come out, but the noble ambition of matrimony? What sends them trooping to watering-places? What keeps them dancing till five o'clock in the morning through a whole mortal season?

column3: Any watcher trained in reading body language would have been prepared to bet that, after the click, someone was going to suggest that they might as well go somewhere and have a few drinks, and then someone else would fancy a meal, and then there was always room for a few more drinks, and then it would be 5 a.m. and the city guards would be respectfully knocking on the University gates and asking if the Archchancellor would care to step down to the cells to identify some alleged wizards who were singing an obscene song in six-part harmony, and perhaps he would also care to bring some money to pay for all the damage.

“It’s sure to be twelve,” Shukhov announced. “The sun’s over the top already.”
“If it is,” the captain retorted, “it’s one o’clock, not twelve.”
“How do you make that out?” Shukhov asked in surprise. “The old folk say the sun is highest at dinnertime.”
“Maybe it was in their day!” the captain snapped back. “Since then it’s been decreed that the sun is highest at one o’clock.”
“Who decreed that?”
“The Soviet government.”

Christmas Eve, and twelve of the clock.
Now they are all on their knees,
An elder said as we sat in a flock
By the embers in hearthside ease.
We pictured the meek mild creatures where
They dwelt in their strawy pen,
Nor did it occur to one of us there
To doubt they were kneeling then.
So fair a fancy few would weave
In these years! Yet, I feel,
If someone said on Christmas Eve,
“Come; see the oxen kneel,
“In the lonely barton by yonder coomb
Our childhood used to know,”
I should go with him in the gloom,
Hoping it might be so.

At 12:11 there was a knock on the door. It was Terry, A could tell. He hadn't known Terry long, but there was something calmer, more patient, that separated Terry's knocks from the rest of the staff. He knocked from genuine politeness, not formality.
Come in, A said, although the lock was on the other side. Terry did.
It's your mother, he said. There's no easy way to say this. Though he had just used the easiest, because A now knew the rest. A’s face froze, as it tried to catch up, as it tried to register the news. Then it crumpled, and while he considered this fresh blow, the tears came.

「請進，」A說，儘管門鎖在另一側。泰瑞進來了。
「是你母親，」他說。「這很難開口。」其實他已經用最簡單的方式說了，因為A立刻明白其餘的一切。A的表情僵住，試圖理解、試圖接受這個消息，然後崩潰，在面對這個新的打擊時，眼淚湧了出來。

A tub was brought in to melt snow for mortar. They heard somebody saying it was twelve o’clock already.
“It’s sure to be twelve,” Shukhov announced. “The sun’s over the top already.”
“If it is,” the captain retorted, “it’s one o’clock, not twelve.”
“How do you make that out?” Shukhov asked in surprise. “The old folk say the sun is highest at dinnertime.”
“Maybe it was in their day!” the captain snapped back. “Since then it’s been decreed that the sun is highest at one o’clock.”
“Who decreed that?”
“The Soviet government.”
